papergraph.title: Single-Cell Landscape of Liver Cancer in Response to Immunotherapy
papergraph.citation: Fei-Yu Diao (2021) Single-Cell Landscape of Liver Cancer in Response to Immunotherapy doi:10.4103/apjon.apjon-2165
papergraph.abstract: Primary liver cancer is the sixth most commonly diagnosed cancer in the world, with 905,677 new cases of liver cancer in 2020, and the third leading cause of cancer death globally. The liver cancer burden in China is substantial, with more than 40% of new liver cancer cases occurred in China. Hepatocellular carcinoma and intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma comprise the majority of liver cancers. Both of them are characterized by extensive intra‐ and inter‐tumor heterogeneity. This may be caused by genomic complexity and the presence of multiple etiological factors. The initiation and progression of cancers, including liver cancer, is a complex evolutionary process, in which natural selection and genetic/epigenetic drift play a key role. A small proportion of tumor cells can survive by acquiring genetic/epigenetic alterations when they are exposed to a microenvironment that is not conducive to tumor growth. This “survival of the fittest” feature, which is common in cancers, leads to intra‐tumor heterogeneity. Just as biodiversity promotes the survival and development of organisms in the ecosystem, intra‐tumor heterogeneity plays a similar role in maintaining tumor survival and promoting tumor metastasis. Additionally, intra‐tumor heterogeneity is the main cause of drug resistance in targeted therapies and severely affects the prognosis of cancer patients. An in‐depth understanding of the biodiversity and clonality of tumors can be of great help at the diagnosis and treatment of tumors. However, the underlying mechanisms that regulate tumor cell evolution are still unknown. Although the existing single‐cell analysis techniques can observe intra‐tumor heterogeneity in solid tumors, these techniques cannot detect interpreting tumor clonality and tumor evolution. Previous studies traced the tumor cell clonality by analyzing somatic mutations in tumor cells. However, most of the somatic mutations observed in these studies are passenger mutations. They are not the drivers of tumor evolution and do not participate in positive selection during the evolution. Therefore, tumor mutational data acquired from single‐cell sequencing cannot be used to reconstruct tumor clonality due to a high false positivity. In a recent study, Marjanovic et al. analyzed the cell status of lung cancer and found that the high‐plasticity cell state drives cell heterogeneity. It is worth noting that the genetic variation in lung cancer evolution did not affect the cell state heterogeneity. These findings suggest that it may be possible to better define the functional clonality of tumor cells and the heterogeneity of their cell states by identifying tumor cell lineages and evolutionary trajectories, as well as tumor cell clusters with transcriptomic similarities at single‐cell level. Previous clinical trials showed that only a small subset of liver cancer patients have a durable response to immune checkpoint inhibitors alone or in combination with anti‐vascular endothelial growth factor therapy. A single‐cell transcriptome analysis conducted by Ma et al. revealed that inter‐tumor heterogeneity was correlated with liver cancer prognosis. It is one of the reasons why liver cancer patients have different reactions to antitumor
papergraph.contribution: Proposes functional clonality and cell-state heterogeneity—rather than somatic mutation-based clonality—as a more accurate framework for understanding tumor evolution and immunotherapy response in liver cancer.
papergraph.limitation: Existing single-cell analysis techniques cannot interpret tumor clonality and tumor evolution; tumor mutational data from single-cell sequencing yield high false positivity for clonality reconstruction due to prevalence of passenger mutations.

papergraph.title: Artificial Intelligence in Cancer Research: Pioneering Biomarker Discovery for Pharmacological Advances
papergraph.citation: Dipti Bhattacharya et al. (2025) Artificial Intelligence in Cancer Research: Pioneering Biomarker Discovery for Pharmacological Advances doi:10.47583/ijpsrr.2025.v85i5.008
papergraph.venue: International journal of pharmaceutical sciences review and research
papergraph.abstract: Molecular heterogeneity of cancer and dynamic nature present major challenges to effective therapy establishment, calling for the highest-level strategies towards biomarker discovery. Herein, this review addresses the paradigm-shifting effect of artificial intelligence (AI) on the discovery of biomarkers to enhance pharmacological specificity and therapeutic efficiency. By merging multi-omics information (genomic, proteomic, transcriptomic) with real-world evidence and clinical records, AI-based algorithms - deep neural networks and ensemble learning models - unveil intricate patterns for anticipating tumor behaviour, drug response, and resistance mechanisms. For instance, AI has determined biomarkers such as FCN3 and CLEC1B in hepatocellular carcinoma with 93 – 98% diagnostic accuracy, whereas recurrent neural networks (RNNs) have mapped angiogenesis-related therapeutic targets in pan-cancer research. These technologies permit stratification of patient subgroups on the basis of molecular signatures to facilitate personalized treatment and drug repurposing via in silico simulations. Significant progress is AI-assisted histopathological image analysis to predict molecular phenotypes and immune checkpoint expression, reducing diagnostic variation by 30 – 40% in validation experiments. Besides, explainable AI (XAI) platforms boost clinical confidence by linking biomarker predictions with tumor microenvironment features such as hypoxia or immune invasion, which translate to survival measures. Nevertheless, there continues to be challenges in standardizing data, model interpretation, and ethically implementing them. Studies indicate that AI-curated biomarker sets improve detection rates in the early stages by 2% in high-mortality cancers (such as ovarian, pancreatic) and predict responses to immunotherapies with AUC-ROC of up to 0.92. Combination of AI with single-cell sequencing and spatial transcriptomics further restricts biomarker specificity to enable dynamic clonal evolution tracking under therapy. Algorithmic bias and restricted diversity in training datasets, however, risk exacerbating healthcare disparities, rendering federated learning and sound validation frameworks critical. In summary, AI-aided biomarker discovery is an oncology paradigm that repairs molecular understanding with clinical application to propel precision pharmacology. papergraph.title: HEPATOCELLULAR CARCINOMA KINOME ATLAS AS AN EMPLOYABLE INSTRUMENTED PERSONALIZED MEDICINE
papergraph.citation: Kipp et al. (2025) HEPATOCELLULAR CARCINOMA KINOME ATLAS AS AN EMPLOYABLE INSTRUMENTED PERSONALIZED MEDICINE doi:10.1101/2025.07.04.25328828
papergraph.abstract: Liver cancer ranks high among cancer death rates and is resistant to most therapies. Studies of protein functionality are limited in patients with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). Here, we constructed an HCC kinome activity atlas and used it to develop personalized medicine technology applicable to profiling protein functionality. We used PamGene PamStation kinome technology that quantified protein kinase activity across hundreds of pathways for HCC tumor and non-tumor regions from both sexes. According to our bioinformatic analyses of kinases, ABL was the most active for men and women with HCC. Next, we employed three ABL inhibitors while running the PamStation, which revealed discernible alterations in ABL and other pathways. We deconvoluted over 500 kinase pathways and generated a personalized medicine (PerMed) score delineating activity levels; results were substantiated in five human hepatocyte cancer cell lines. Our work establishes an HCC kinome atlas and demonstrates PamStations potential for precision medicine applications.
improvements: No explicit limitation extracted.
papergraph.contribution: Establishment of an HCC kinome activity atlas and demonstration of PamStation's utility for precision medicine applications via kinase pathway deconvolution and inhibitor response profiling.

papergraph.title: Impact of Comorbidity and Drug Use Patterns on Recurrence and Mortality Risks in Primary Hepatocellular Carcinoma: A Chinese Cohort Network Analysis Study
papergraph.citation: Yang, B. et al. (2025) Impact of Comorbidity and Drug Use Patterns on Recurrence and Mortality Risks in Primary Hepatocellular Carcinoma: A Chinese Cohort Network Analysis Study doi:10.1101/2025.07.05.25330803
papergraph.abstract:
Background & AimsComorbidities in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) impair liver function, limit treatment choices, disrupt drug metabolism, and adversely impact survival. This study identifies comorbidity and multidrug combination patterns to evaluate their relationships with HCC outcomes.

MethodsThis cohort study comprised 15,998 patients (62.7% male, age at HCC diagnosis: 69.2 [IQR: 58.7-78.7] years old) from the Hong Kong Hospital Authority, enrolled between January 2008 and December 2019 with follow-up until May 2024. Primary endpoints were HCC recurrence and liver cancer-related mortality, while secondary endpoints included cancer-related mortality, non-cancer-related mortality, and all-cause mortality. We identified the most frequent double, triple, quadruple, quintuple patterns of both comorbidities and multidrug combinations using UpSet plots, then examined their associations with clinical outcomes using network analyses. Risk factors were evaluated through Cox proportional hazards regression models, with robustness confirmed via sensitivity analyses and subgroup assessments.

ResultsIn this cohort, 93.0% of patients had at least one pre-existing comorbidity (mean 3.0 per patient), of whom 87.8% (N=13,084) had 1-5 comorbidities. Diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and existing chronic liver diseases were the predominant comorbidities across all combinations, followed by colorectal cancer and renal diseases. Males demonstrated greater comorbidity burden, particularly involving metabolic disorders and advanced liver diseases. Notably, males with moderate-to-severe liver diseases had elevated HCC recurrence risk compared to females. Multidrug use rate among those with HCC recurrence was 79.5% (N=9999), of whom 25.7% with double-drug exposure, 13.8% with triple-drug exposure. Anti-diabetic drugs, ACEI/ARB, and diuretics for heart failure, anti-cancer drugs, and NSAIDs were the most common combination use. Both adjusted Cox regression models and subgroup analyses confirmed multi-comorbidity as a robust predictor of clinical outcomes.

ConclusionOur gender and age-specific characterization of comorbidity and multidrug patterns in HCC provides clinicians and patients with actionable insights to guide proactive management and patient-centered care, enabling earlier comorbidity detection and prevention strategies.
papergraph.conclusion: Our gender and age-specific characterization of comorbidity and multidrug patterns in HCC provides clinicians and patients with actionable insights to guide proactive management and patient-centered care, enabling earlier comorbidity detection and prevention strategies.

papergraph.title: Butyrate modifies epigenetic and immune pathways in peripheral mononuclear cells from children with neurodevelopmental disorders associated with chromatin dysregulation
papergraph.citation: Hayes et al. (2025) Butyrate modifies epigenetic and immune pathways in peripheral mononuclear cells from children with neurodevelopmental disorders associated with chromatin dysregulation doi:10.1101/2025.07.03.25330764
papergraph.abstract: Pathogenic DNA variants in chromatin-related genes cause an important minority of neurodevelopmental disorders (NDDs). Epigenetic mechanisms, including chromatin regulation, are associated with NDD etiopathogenesis. Therapeutic strategies targeting chromatin dysregulation, such as histone deacetylase inhibition with butyrate, show promise; however, its effects remain poorly understood. We performed single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) on 101,539 peripheral immune cells from four children with functionally impairing NDDs (median age 11.2 years,; 3 females): three with de novo pathogenic variants in chromatin-related genes (KMT2D, CHD7, and MECP2), and one without a monogenic diagnosis (non-monogenic), compared with two sex-matched healthy controls (median age 12.5 years, 1 female). Patient cells also underwent scRNA-seq after in vitro butyrate treatment. We identified 136 to 6909 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) across patient comparisons. Untreated patient cells showed dysregulation of ribosomal and immune pathways compared to controls. KMT2D, CHD7, and the non-monogenic patients exhibited downregulation of ribosomal pathways, and upregulation of immune pathways, whereas the MECP2 patient displayed upregulation of ribosomal pathways and mixed regulation of immune pathways. Butyrate largely reversed these pathways, normalizing ribosomal and immune pathways in KMT2D, CHD7, and non-monogenic cells, with partial effects in MECP2. Overall, butyrate induced up-regulation of ribosome, GTPase, cytoskeletal, mitochondrial pathways, and down-regulation of epigenetic and immune pathways. This is the first study to demonstrate that butyrate modulates epigenetic and immune gene networks in monogenic and non-monogenic NDDs. The shared ribosomal-immune RNA signature suggests a common downstream mechanism of chromatin dysregulation, positioning butyrate as a promising therapeutic modulator across diverse NDDs.

papergraph.title: RBMX2: A Pivotal Regulator Linking Mycobacterium bovis Infection to Epithelial-Mesenchymal Transition and Lung Cancer Progression
papergraph.citation: Wang, C. et al. (2025) RBMX2: A Pivotal Regulator Linking Mycobacterium bovis Infection to Epithelial-Mesenchymal Transition and Lung Cancer Progression doi:10.1101/2025.04.15.648920
papergraph.abstract:
Tuberculosis (TB) is a complex disease that arises from the dynamic interplay among the pathogen, host, and environmental factors. In 2022, TB affected approximately 10.6 million people worldwide, resulting in 1.3 million deaths. In regions with a high burden of zoonotic TB, Mycobacterium bovis (M. bovis) accounts for nearly 10% of human TB cases. The immune evasion strategies and latent nature of Mycobacterium tuberculosis (M. tb) pose significant challenges to understanding the host immune response to TB infection. In this study, we identify RNA-binding motif protein X-linked 2 (RBMX2) as a novel host factor that may facilitate M. bovis infection. However, the molecular mechanisms and functional roles of RBMX2 during M. bovis infection remain poorly understood.

Our findings show that RBMX2 is significantly upregulated across multiple cell types following infection, including embryonic bovine lung (EBL) cells, bovine macrophage (BoMac) cells, bovine pulmonary alveolar primary cells, and human alveolar epithelial cells (A549). Using a combination of global transcriptomic sequencing, proteomic profiling, cell adhesion assays, ChIP-PCR, and Western blotting, we demonstrate that RBMX2 suppresses cell adhesion and tight junction formation in EBL cells while promoting M. bovis adhesion and invasion through activation of the p65 signaling pathway. Importantly, integrated analyses of transcriptomic, proteomic, and metabolomic data reveal that RBMX2 plays a key role in regulating epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT), a cellular process closely associated with cancer progression.

To further explore the potential link between RBMX2 and cancer, we analyzed the TIMER2.0 database and found elevated RBMX2 expression in lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD) and lung squamous cell carcinoma (LUSC) tissues compared to normal lung tissues. These findings were corroborated by immunofluorescence staining. Using an M. bovis-infected BoMac-induced EBL-EMT model, we show that RBMX2 promotes EMT through activation of the p65/MMP-9 pathway following infection. Additionally, we validated the EMT process in human lung epithelial cancer cells (H1299), further supporting the involvement of RBMX2 in this transition.

Collectively, our study identifies RBMX2 as a novel host factor that may enhance M. bovis infection and drive infection-induced EMT. These findings provide new insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying TB pathogenesis and suggest that RBMX2 could serve as a potential target for the development of TB vaccines and therapeutic strategies against TB-associated lung cancer.

papergraph.title: Cancer-associated metabolic alterations facilitated by astrocyte glycogenolysis mediate neuropathic pain chronification
papergraph.citation: Park, J. et al. (2025) Cancer-associated metabolic alterations facilitated by astrocyte glycogenolysis mediate neuropathic pain chronification doi:10.1101/2025.05.27.655245
papergraph.abstract: Chronic pain remains a major unmet medical challenge, yet the metabolic checkpoints that govern its persistence are poorly defined. Astrocytes are increasingly recognized as chemically programmable hubs that tune neuronal excitability through metabolic circuits. Building on reports that astrocyte-neuron lactate shuttling (ANLS) in the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) supports chronic pain, we asked how astrocytic metabolic states evolve over the course of pain chronification. Using untargeted metabolomics of the ACC combined with GFAP-RiboTag-based astrocyte-specific transcriptomics, we provide a time-resolved map of astrocytic metabolism across the transition from acute nociception to chronic neuropathic pain. This analysis reveals a biphasic glycogen program--an acute glycogenolysis-triggered glycogen supercompensation--that culminates in the emergence of a Warburg-like metabolic signature with tissue acidification associated with sustained lactate shuttling and persistent circuit activation. Using glycogen phosphorylase inhibitors (GPI-1, GPI-2) as pharmacological probes, we show that early glycogenolysis blockade attenuates this Warburg-like shift, partially normalizes ACC metabolic signatures, and reduces long-lasting mechanical hypersensitivity, without impairing acute nociceptive sensitization. These findings identify astrocytic metabolic reprogramming as a pharmacologically tractable circuit-level process and nominate glycogenolysis as an upstream biochemical gate and potential therapeutic control point in neuropathic pain.
strengths: identifies astrocytic metabolic reprogramming as pharmacologically tractable
uses glycogen phosphorylase inhibitors as mechanistic precision probes
provides time-resolved map of astrocytic metabolism
improvements: move beyond correlation to mechanism-first intervention

position glycogenolysis as therapeutically actionable leverage point
papergraph.contribution: Astrocytic glycogenolysis acts as an upstream biochemical gate and pharmacologically tractable control point that drives metabolic reprogramming toward a Warburg-like signature, stabilizing hyperexcitable circuit dynamics and mediating neuropathic pain chronification.

papergraph.title: Spatiotemporal variations in the prevalence and determinants of maternal delivery at home in Bangladesh, 2012-2022: A population-based cross-sectional study
papergraph.citation: Islam et al. (2025) Spatiotemporal variations in the prevalence and determinants of maternal delivery at home in Bangladesh, 2012-2022: A population-based cross-sectional study doi:10.1101/2025.07.05.25330941
papergraph.abstract:
BackgroundDespite significant progress in reducing maternal and neonatal mortality, home delivery remains a substantial public health challenge in Bangladesh and many other low- and middle-income countries. While the proportion of home deliveries has markedly decreased in the past decade, pronounced disparities persist across geographic, socioeconomic, and demographic groups. A nuanced understanding of the prevalence and determinants of maternal home delivery is key to designing targeted interventions. This study examines national and subnational variations in maternal home delivery and associated factors in Bangladesh.
MethodsWe analyzed data from the Multiple Indicator Cluster Surveys (2012-13 and 2019) and the Bangladesh Demographic and Health Survey (2022), covering 20,770 ever-married women aged 15-49 who gave birth in the preceding two years. District-level prevalence, descriptive statistics, and multivariable logistic regression were used to assess trends and determinants.
ResultsHome delivery prevalence declined from 68% in 2012-13 to 35% in 2022. Disparities remain: divisions such as Barisal (48.9%), Chattogram, Sylhet, and Mymensingh showed higher rates, while Dhaka and Khulna had the lowest. At the subnational level, remote areas like Bandarban, Rangamati, and Bhola exhibited higher prevalence. The logistic regression analysis identified several significant predictors, such as women with no formal education, limited ANC visits ([≤]3), rural residence, lower wealth status, multiparity ([≥]3 children), and lack of media exposure were more likely to deliver at home.
ConclusionsDespite marked improvement, persistent geographic and socioeconomic inequities highlight the need for targeted interventions. Strengthening healthcare infrastructure in underserved regions, promoting maternal health awareness, scaling up ANC utilization, and reducing financial barriers through subsidies and incentive programs can help further decrease home deliveries. Future research should explore cultural and religious factors to inform context-specific policies for equitable facility-based childbirth.
papergraph.conclusion: Despite marked improvement, persistent geographic and socioeconomic inequities highlight the need for targeted interventions. Strengthening healthcare infrastructure in underserved regions, promoting maternal health awareness, scaling up ANC utilization, and reducing financial barriers through subsidies and incentive programs can help further decrease home deliveries. Future research should explore cultural and religious factors to inform context-specific policies for equitable facility-based childbirth.

papergraph.title: Precursors of Sea Star Wasting: Immune and Microbial Disruption During Initial Disease Outbreak in Southeast Alaska
papergraph.citation: McCracken, A. et al. (2025) Precursors of Sea Star Wasting: Immune and Microbial Disruption During Initial Disease Outbreak in Southeast Alaska doi:10.1101/2025.07.05.662347
papergraph.abstract: Sea Star Wasting Disease (SSW) has devastated sea star populations along the North American Pacific coast since 2013, yet the mechanisms of disease progression, particularly in natural environments, remain unclear. Here we integrate transcriptomic and microbial data from wild Pycnopodia helianthoides sampled across sites affected and unaffected by SSW in southeast Alaska during the initial outbreak recorded in the region in 2016. Individuals exposed to SSW but lacking visible symptoms showed elevated expression of complement system components, pathogen recognition genes, immune regulatory and cell death pathways. Alongside signs of immune activation, genes involved in maintaining extracellular matrix composition, tissue remodeling, and cell adhesion were differentially expressed, indicating early disruption of tissue homeostasis preceding visible wasting symptoms. Gene ontology analysis revealed enrichment of immune response, cell-cell adhesion, response to oxygen levels and nervous system regulatory pathways. Furthermore, network analyses revealed differentially abundant microbes in Exposed individuals--notably Vibrio spp.--were highly correlated with immune response, tissue integrity, stress and detoxification genes in network modules. Together, our findings offer insight into early host-pathogen dynamics in wild populations, underscoring putative links between immune activation and microbial community shifts with the onset of SSW disease.

papergraph.title: Multipartite coevolution shapes plant apoplastic immunity against rice blast fungus
papergraph.citation: Takeda, T. et al. (2025) Multipartite coevolution shapes plant apoplastic immunity against rice blast fungus doi:10.1101/2025.07.03.663104
papergraph.abstract: β-1,3-glucan-binding proteins (GBPs) play crucial roles in cell wall integrity, host- pathogen interactions, and stress responses in fungi, making them essential for fungal survival and virulence. Here, we show that a GBP of the causal agent for blast fungus, Magnaporthe oryzae, is involved in multipartite coevolutionary arms race with rice (Oryza sativa). The rice thaumatin-like pathogenesis-related protein 5 (OsPR5) binds to M. oryzae GBP and sequesters it from the fungal cell wall to confer immunity, but this interaction is counteracted by thaumatin-binding proteins secreted by the fungus to suppress apoplastic immunity. Moreover, a rice receptor kinase located at the cell surface has evolved a thaumatin-like domain to detect the fungal GBP and activate immunity. Our findings reveal that GBPs and thaumatin-domain-containing proteins are engaged in intricate coevolutionary dynamics in the rice apoplast. Given the conservation of thaumatin across plants, we propose a general defense mechanism against fungal pathogens.

papergraph.title: A Pipeline for Screening Small Molecule-Enhanced Protein Stability in A Bacterial Orphan Receptor
papergraph.citation: Siclari et al. (2025) A Pipeline for Screening Small Molecule-Enhanced Protein Stability in A Bacterial Orphan Receptor doi:10.1101/2025.07.03.663076
papergraph.abstract: Bacterial one-component signaling proteins integrate sensory and gene regulation functions within the same polypeptide, creating powerful natural sensors of environmental conditions which can also be adapted into powerful tools for synthetic biology and biotechnology. A key sensor motif within many of these proteins is the Per- ARNT-Sim (PAS) domain, known for its conserved fold yet highly divergent sequences, allowing for a broad range of ligands to control PAS protein function by changes in small molecule binding occupancy or configuration. This diversity introduces a challenging step - identification of ligands for "orphan" PAS proteins which show signatures of ligand binding but no copurifying high-affinity bound small molecules - into characterization and engineering of such proteins. In this study, we characterized CU228, a putative PAS-HTH transcription factor from Candidatus Solibacter usitatus, as a novel model system for searching for novel ligands by small molecule stabilization. Bioinformatics and structural analyses predicted a PAS domain with an Trp-rich internal cavity, suggesting potential small molecule interactions. Using a [~]760 compound fragment library, differential scanning fluorimetry identified three ligands (KG-96, KG- 408, and KG-484) that substantially increased CU228 thermal stability with {Delta}Tm values up to +10{degrees}C. Microfluidic modulation spectroscopy (MMS) revealed ligand-induced preservation of -helical and {beta}-sheet integrity under thermal stress. Saturation transfer difference NMR confirmed direct binding of all three ligands and enabled estimation of micromolar-range dissociation constants, consistent with expected fragment-level affinity. Our findings expand the analytical toolbox for probing protein-ligand interactions in flexible, signal-responsive systems, laying the groundwork for designing synthetic chemogenetic variants of one-component transcription factors.

keynote.core_problem: Existing single-cell analysis techniques cannot reconstruct tumor clonality or trace tumor evolution due to high false positivity from passenger mutations in mutational data; intra- and inter-tumor heterogeneity impedes immunotherapy response and drives drug resistance.

gap_signals.text: Tumor mutational data acquired from single-cell sequencing cannot be used to reconstruct tumor clonality due to a high false positivity caused by prevalence of passenger mutations.

keynote.title: Impact of Comorbidity and Drug Use Patterns on Recurrence and Mortality Risks in Primary Hepatocellular Carcinoma: A Chinese Cohort Network Analysis Study
keynote.core_problem: How comorbidity patterns and multidrug use combinations relate to HCC recurrence and mortality risks, with attention to gender and age stratification.
contributions: First study to investigate age-, sex-, and CCI-stratified network of multimorbidity and multi-drug exposure in HCC
Characterized gender- and age-specific comorbidity and multidrug patterns associated with HCC outcomes
Identified predominant comorbidity combinations (e.g., diabetes, hypertension, chronic liver disease) and drug combinations (e.g., anti-diabetics, ACEI/ARB, diuretics, anti-cancer drugs, NSAIDs)
Demonstrated that multi-comorbidity is a robust predictor of HCC recurrence and mortality across Cox models and subgroup analyses
Revealed elevated HCC recurrence risk in males with moderate-to-severe liver disease compared to females
methods: Cohort study design with 15,998 HCC patients from Hong Kong Hospital Authority (2008–2019, follow-up to May 2024)
UpSet plots to identify frequent double- to quintuple-combination patterns of comorbidities and multidrug exposures
Network analyses to examine associations between comorbidity/multidrug patterns and clinical outcomes
Cox proportional hazards regression models for risk factor evaluation
Sensitivity analyses and subgroup assessments for robustness validation
experiments_or_evidence: 93.0% of patients had ≥1 pre-existing comorbidity (mean 3.0 per patient); 87.8% had 1–5 comorbidities
Multidrug use rate among patients with HCC recurrence was 79.5% (N=9999), with 25.7% on double-drug and 13.8% on triple-drug regimens
Diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and chronic liver diseases were predominant comorbidities; anti-diabetic drugs, ACEI/ARB, diuretics, anti-cancer drugs, and NSAIDs were most common drug classes
Males showed greater comorbidity burden—especially metabolic disorders and advanced liver disease
Males with moderate-to-severe liver disease had elevated HCC recurrence risk vs. females
Multi-comorbidity remained a robust predictor across adjusted Cox models and subgroup analyses
assumptions: Cox proportional hazards assumption holds for survival modeling
Comorbidity and drug exposure data recorded in electronic health records are accurate and complete
Observed associations reflect causal or clinically meaningful relationships (not explicitly validated)
limitations: Study population limited to Hong Kong Chinese cohort — limits generalizability to other ethnicities or healthcare systems
No mention of treatment modality stratification (e.g., resection vs. TACE vs. systemic therapy) in analyses
Drug exposure defined by prescription records — no pharmacokinetic or adherence data
Comorbidity burden measured via count and pattern, not severity scoring beyond CCI mention (CCI stratification noted but not detailed in methods/results)

gap_signals.text: Comorbidity burden measured via count and pattern, not severity scoring beyond CCI mention (CCI stratification noted but not detailed in methods/results)
Drug exposure defined by prescription records — no pharmacokinetic or adherence data
No mention of treatment modality stratification (e.g., resection vs. TACE vs. systemic therapy) in analyses
Study population limited to Hong Kong Chinese cohort — limits generalizability to other ethnicities or healthcare systems

To the best of our knowledge, there is no research study investigating age, sex, and CCI stratified network of multimorbidity and multi-drugs exposure, nor exploring the associations between co-comorbidities or co-drugs exposure and follow-up HCC recurrence and mortality risk.

datasets_or_materials: Hong Kong Hospital Authority electronic health record database
Cohort of 15,998 primary HCC patients diagnosed Jan 2008–Dec 2019, followed until May 2024

important_claims.claim: Multi-comorbidity is a robust predictor of HCC recurrence and mortality.
important_claims.evidence: Confirmed via adjusted Cox regression models and subgroup analyses.

important_claims.claim: Males with moderate-to-severe liver disease have elevated HCC recurrence risk compared to females.
important_claims.evidence: Noted as a 'notable' finding in results; supported by subgroup analysis (implied but not quantified in abstract).

important_claims.claim: Anti-diabetic drugs, ACEI/ARB, diuretics, anti-cancer drugs, and NSAIDs are the most common multidrug combinations in HCC patients.
important_claims.evidence: Stated as observed pattern among patients with HCC recurrence (79.5% multidrug users).

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a methodological template for network-based multimorbidity and polypharmacy analysis in oncology cohorts; highlights need for sex- and age-stratified comorbidity burden assessment in liver cancer prognostication.

keynotes.title: Butyrate modifies epigenetic and immune pathways in peripheral mononuclear cells from children with neurodevelopmental disorders associated with chromatin dysregulation

keynote.title: Butyrate modifies epigenetic and immune pathways in peripheral mononuclear cells from children with neurodevelopmental disorders associated with chromatin dysregulation
keynote.core_problem: Poor understanding of how butyrate — a histone deacetylase inhibitor — affects epigenetic and immune pathways in children with neurodevelopmental disorders (NDDs) linked to chromatin dysregulation, including both monogenic (e.g., KMT2D, CHD7, MECP2) and non-monogenic cases.
contributions: First demonstration that butyrate modulates epigenetic and immune gene networks in both monogenic and non-monogenic NDDs using scRNA-seq.
Identification of a shared ribosomal-immune RNA signature across multiple chromatin-related NDDs, suggesting a common downstream mechanism of chromatin dysregulation.
Characterization of divergent baseline pathway dysregulation (e.g., ribosomal vs. immune) across distinct genetic subtypes, and differential rescue by butyrate.
methods: Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) on peripheral mononuclear cells.
In vitro butyrate treatment of patient-derived cells followed by scRNA-seq.
Differential gene expression analysis (DEG identification).
Pathway-level enrichment analysis (e.g., ribosomal, immune, mitochondrial, epigenetic pathways).
experiments_or_evidence: scRNA-seq on 101,539 peripheral immune cells from four children with NDDs (KMT2D, CHD7, MECP2, non-monogenic) and two sex-matched healthy controls.
Comparison of untreated patient cells vs. controls revealing 136–6909 DEGs and dysregulated ribosomal/immune pathways.
Post-butyrate scRNA-seq showing normalization of ribosomal and immune pathways in KMT2D, CHD7, and non-monogenic cells; partial effects in MECP2.
Pathway-level shifts: butyrate induced upregulation of ribosome, GTPase, cytoskeletal, mitochondrial pathways; downregulation of epigenetic and immune pathways.
assumptions: Peripheral mononuclear cells reflect relevant epigenetic/immune dysregulation present in CNS-related NDD pathophysiology.
In vitro butyrate treatment recapitulates therapeutically relevant molecular effects observed in vivo.
scRNA-seq-derived pathway signatures are biologically interpretable and generalizable across small-N patient subgroups.
limitations: Small sample size (n=4 patients, n=2 controls), limiting statistical power and generalizability.
Lack of functional validation (e.g., protein-level, enzymatic, or phenotypic assays) for observed transcriptional changes.
No longitudinal or in vivo data; findings based solely on ex vivo cell culture and short-term butyrate exposure.
Absence of isogenic controls or variant-specific mechanistic dissection (e.g., no rescue experiments with corrected lines).

gap_signals.text: Small sample size (n=4 patients, n=2 controls), limiting statistical power and generalizability.
Absence of isogenic controls or variant-specific mechanistic dissection (e.g., no rescue experiments with corrected lines).
Lack of functional validation (e.g., protein-level, enzymatic, or phenotypic assays) for observed transcriptional changes.
No longitudinal or in vivo data; findings based solely on ex vivo cell culture and short-term butyrate exposure.
Unclear whether butyrate’s effects are uniform or subtype-specific — this study reveals differential responses (e.g., MECP2 vs. KMT2D/CHD7).
Lack of evidence for a shared molecular signature (ribosomal-immune dysregulation) across genetically heterogeneous chromatin-related NDDs.
No prior study has applied scRNA-seq to assess butyrate’s effects on epigenetic and immune pathways in NDD patient-derived peripheral cells.

datasets_or_materials: scRNA-seq data from peripheral mononuclear cells of four children with NDDs (KMT2D, CHD7, MECP2, non-monogenic) and two healthy controls.
In vitro butyrate-treated scRNA-seq data from same patient cells.

important_claims.claim: Butyrate normalizes ribosomal and immune pathways in KMT2D, CHD7, and non-monogenic NDD cells, but only partially in MECP2 cells.
important_claims.evidence: Post-butyrate scRNA-seq showed reversal of baseline dysregulation in ribosomal and immune pathways for KMT2D, CHD7, and non-monogenic cases; MECP2 exhibited mixed and incomplete rescue.

important_claims.claim: Untreated NDD cells show a shared ribosomal-immune RNA signature despite genetic heterogeneity.
important_claims.evidence: KMT2D, CHD7, and non-monogenic patients all showed ribosomal downregulation and immune upregulation; MECP2 was an exception (ribosomal upregulation), suggesting the signature is common but not universal.

important_claims.claim: Butyrate induces broad transcriptional reprogramming beyond epigenetic targets — including upregulation of ribosome, GTPase, cytoskeletal, and mitochondrial pathways.
important_claims.evidence: Pathway enrichment analysis of butyrate-treated cells consistently identified these upregulated functional modules across responsive samples.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — provides a scRNA-seq resource and analytical framework for investigating chromatin-targeting therapeutics in rare NDDs; identifies candidate convergent pathways (ribosomal-immune axis) for biomarker and mechanistic follow-up; highlights need for subtype-stratified therapeutic evaluation.

keynotes.title: RBMX2: A Pivotal Regulator Linking Mycobacterium bovis Infection to Epithelial-Mesenchymal Transition and Lung Cancer Progression

keynote.title: RBMX2: A Pivotal Regulator Linking Mycobacterium bovis Infection to Epithelial-Mesenchymal Transition and Lung Cancer Progression
keynote.core_problem: Poor understanding of host molecular mechanisms enabling Mycobacterium bovis infection and its potential role in driving epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and lung cancer progression.
contributions: Identification of RBMX2 as a novel host factor upregulated during M. bovis infection.
Demonstration that RBMX2 suppresses cell adhesion and tight junction formation while promoting M. bovis adhesion/invasion via p65 signaling.
Establishment of RBMX2's role in regulating EMT during M. bovis infection, mediated through the p65/MMP-9 pathway.
Correlation of elevated RBMX2 expression with lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD) and lung squamous cell carcinoma (LUSC) using TIMER2.0 and immunofluorescence.
methods: Global transcriptomic sequencing
Proteomic profiling
Cell adhesion assays
ChIP-PCR
Western blotting
Integrated multi-omics analysis (transcriptomic, proteomic, metabolomic)
Immunofluorescence staining
Analysis of TIMER2.0 database
experiments_or_evidence: RBMX2 upregulation observed across multiple infected cell types: EBL, BoMac, bovine pulmonary alveolar primary cells, and human A549 cells.
RBMX2 suppression of cell adhesion and tight junctions in EBL cells.
RBMX2 promotion of M. bovis adhesion/invasion via p65 activation.
RBMX2-mediated EMT induction in an M. bovis-infected BoMac-induced EBL-EMT model.
Validation of EMT in human H1299 lung cancer cells.
Elevated RBMX2 expression in LUAD and LUSC tissues vs. normal lung tissue (TIMER2.0 + immunofluorescence).

gap_signals.text: The molecular mechanisms and functional roles of RBMX2 during M. bovis infection remain poorly understood.

important_claims.claim: RBMX2 is significantly upregulated across multiple cell types following M. bovis infection.
important_claims.evidence: Observed in EBL, BoMac, bovine pulmonary alveolar primary cells, and human A549 cells.

important_claims.claim: RBMX2 promotes M. bovis adhesion and invasion via activation of the p65 signaling pathway.
important_claims.evidence: Demonstrated using global transcriptomic sequencing, proteomic profiling, cell adhesion assays, ChIP-PCR, and Western blotting in EBL cells.

important_claims.claim: RBMX2 regulates epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) during M. bovis infection.
important_claims.evidence: Revealed by integrated transcriptomic, proteomic, and metabolomic analyses; validated in BoMac-induced EBL-EMT model and human H1299 cells.

important_claims.claim: RBMX2 expression is elevated in lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD) and lung squamous cell carcinoma (LUSC).
important_claims.evidence: Identified via TIMER2.0 database analysis and corroborated by immunofluorescence staining.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a candidate host factor (RBMX2) and associated pathway (p65/MMP-9) for mechanistic studies of infection-driven EMT and TB-associated lung carcinogenesis; suggests utility in cross-species (bovine/human) models of mycobacterial pathogenesis.

keynotes.title: Cancer-associated metabolic alterations facilitated by astrocyte glycogenolysis mediate neuropathic pain chronification

keynote.title: Cancer-associated metabolic alterations facilitated by astrocyte glycogenolysis mediate neuropathic pain chronification
keynote.core_problem: Poorly defined metabolic checkpoints governing the persistence (chronification) of chronic neuropathic pain.
contributions: Time-resolved map of astrocytic metabolism in the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) across acute-to-chronic pain transition
Discovery of a biphasic astrocytic glycogen program: acute glycogenolysis followed by glycogen supercompensation
Identification of a Warburg-like metabolic signature with tissue acidification and sustained lactate shuttling in chronic pain
Demonstration that early pharmacological blockade of glycogenolysis attenuates metabolic reprogramming and mechanical hypersensitivity
methods: Untargeted metabolomics of the ACC
GFAP-RiboTag-based astrocyte-specific transcriptomics
Pharmacological inhibition using glycogen phosphorylase inhibitors (GPI-1, GPI-2)
experiments_or_evidence: Time-resolved metabolic and transcriptomic profiling across pain chronification
Pharmacological intervention with GPI-1/GPI-2 showing attenuation of Warburg-like shift and mechanical hypersensitivity
Correlation of glycogenolysis timing with emergence of sustained lactate shuttling and circuit activation
assumptions: Astrocyte-neuron lactate shuttling (ANLS) in ACC is mechanistically relevant to chronic pain
Glycogenolysis is an upstream, causal driver—not merely correlative—of metabolic and behavioral chronification

gap_signals.text: How astrocytic metabolic states evolve over the course of pain chronification was unknown.
Metabolic checkpoints governing persistence of chronic pain are poorly defined.
No identified upstream biochemical gate amenable to pharmacological intervention in neuropathic pain chronification.

important_claims.claim: Astrocytic metabolic reprogramming is a pharmacologically tractable circuit-level process.
important_claims.evidence: Early glycogenolysis blockade with GPI-1/GPI-2 attenuates Warburg-like shift, normalizes ACC metabolic signatures, and reduces mechanical hypersensitivity without impairing acute nociception.

important_claims.claim: Glycogenolysis acts as an upstream biochemical gate in neuropathic pain chronification.
important_claims.evidence: Inhibition of glycogenolysis prevents downstream Warburg-like metabolic signature, tissue acidification, sustained lactate shuttling, and persistent circuit activation.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides time-resolved astrocyte-specific metabolic and transcriptomic resources from ACC during pain chronification; introduces glycogenolysis inhibition as a testable intervention paradigm for circuit-level metabolic dysregulation in chronic pain models.

keynotes.title: Spatiotemporal variations in the prevalence and determinants of maternal delivery at home in Bangladesh, 2012-2022: A population-based cross-sectional study

keynote.title: Spatiotemporal variations in the prevalence and determinants of maternal delivery at home in Bangladesh, 2012-2022: A population-based cross-sectional study
keynote.core_problem: Persistent spatiotemporal disparities in home delivery despite national decline; inequitable access to facility-based childbirth across geographic, socioeconomic, and demographic groups in Bangladesh.
contributions: Quantified national decline in home delivery prevalence (68% → 35%) from 2012–13 to 2022
Mapped subnational variation—identified high-prevalence divisions (Barisal, Chattogram, Sylhet, Mymensingh) and districts (Bandarban, Rangamati, Bhola)
Identified robust socioeconomic and health-system determinants via multivariable logistic regression
methods: Population-based cross-sectional analysis using nationally representative survey data
District-level prevalence estimation and descriptive statistics
Multivariable logistic regression to assess determinants of home delivery
experiments_or_evidence: Trend analysis across three nationally representative surveys: MICS 2012–13, MICS 2019, BDHS 2022
Regression results showing significant associations for no formal education, ≤3 ANC visits, rural residence, lower wealth status, multiparity (≥3), and lack of media exposure
assumptions: Home delivery status is accurately self-reported in surveys
Temporal trends reflect real-world change rather than survey methodology differences
Associations identified are stable across survey rounds despite varying sampling designs
limitations: Lack of qualitative or contextual data on cultural and religious factors (explicitly noted as unexplored)
No assessment of service quality, provider availability, or transportation barriers

gap_signals.text: Cross-sectional design precludes causal inference
Lack of qualitative or contextual data on cultural and religious factors (explicitly noted as unexplored)

Future research should explore cultural and religious factors to inform context-specific policies for equitable facility-based childbirth.

important_claims.claim: Home delivery prevalence declined from 68% in 2012–13 to 35% in 2022.
important_claims.evidence: Analysis of MICS 2012–13, MICS 2019, and BDHS 2022 data covering 20,770 women.

important_claims.claim: Women with no formal education, ≤3 ANC visits, rural residence, lower wealth status, multiparity (≥3), and lack of media exposure were significantly more likely to deliver at home.
important_claims.evidence: Multivariable logistic regression results reported in the Results section.

important_claims.claim: Geographic disparities persist, with Barisal division (48.9%) and remote districts (Bandarban, Rangamati, Bhola) showing markedly higher home delivery prevalence.
important_claims.evidence: District-level prevalence analysis presented in Results.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides benchmark spatiotemporal estimates and validated socioeconomic determinants for comparative equity analyses in LMIC maternal health; survey data sources and regression framework are reusable for replication or extension.

keynotes.title: Precursors of Sea Star Wasting: Immune and Microbial Disruption During Initial Disease Outbreak in Southeast Alaska

keynote.title: Precursors of Sea Star Wasting: Immune and Microbial Disruption During Initial Disease Outbreak in Southeast Alaska
keynote.core_problem: Unclear mechanisms of Sea Star Wasting Disease (SSW) progression in natural environments, especially during early (pre-symptomatic) stages.
contributions: Identification of pre-symptomatic immune activation (complement, pathogen recognition, immune regulation, cell death) in exposed but asymptomatic sea stars.
Detection of early disruption in tissue homeostasis (extracellular matrix, tissue remodeling, cell adhesion) prior to visible wasting.
Revelation of coordinated shifts in microbial community (e.g., Vibrio spp.) correlated with host immune and tissue integrity gene expression via network analysis.
Integration of transcriptomic and microbial data from wild Pycnopodia helianthoides across affected/unaffected sites during the 2016 southeast Alaska outbreak.
methods: Transcriptomic profiling (RNA-seq) of wild Pycnopodia helianthoides
Microbial community analysis (16S rRNA or similar, inferred from 'differentially abundant microbes')
Gene ontology (GO) enrichment analysis
Co-expression or correlation-based network analysis linking microbes and host genes
experiments_or_evidence: Comparison of gene expression in SSW-exposed vs. unexposed (unaffected-site) wild sea stars
Differential expression analysis revealing upregulation of complement, pathogen recognition, immune regulation, and cell death pathways in exposed individuals
Differential expression of extracellular matrix, tissue remodeling, and cell adhesion genes in exposed individuals
GO enrichment showing immune response, cell-cell adhesion, oxygen response, and nervous system regulatory pathways
Network modules correlating Vibrio spp. abundance with immune, tissue integrity, stress, and detoxification genes

gap_signals.text: Prior work lacks integration of transcriptomic and microbial data from wild populations during initial outbreak phases.
Mechanisms of disease progression, particularly in natural environments, remain unclear.

datasets_or_materials: Wild Pycnopodia helianthoides specimens from southeast Alaska (2016), stratified by SSW exposure status (exposed/asymptomatic vs. unexposed/unaffected sites)

important_claims.claim: Exposed but asymptomatic sea stars show elevated expression of complement system components, pathogen recognition genes, immune regulatory and cell death pathways.
important_claims.evidence: Transcriptomic data from wild Pycnopodia helianthoides sampled across affected and unaffected sites in southeast Alaska during the 2016 outbreak.

important_claims.claim: Early disruption of tissue homeostasis (extracellular matrix, tissue remodeling, cell adhesion) precedes visible wasting symptoms.
important_claims.evidence: Differential expression of genes involved in extracellular matrix composition, tissue remodeling, and cell adhesion in exposed but asymptomatic individuals.

important_claims.claim: Differentially abundant microbes—including Vibrio spp.—are highly correlated with immune response, tissue integrity, stress and detoxification genes.
important_claims.evidence: Network analyses revealing correlations between Vibrio spp. abundance and host gene modules in exposed individuals.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a multi-omics framework (host transcriptome + microbiome) for detecting pre-symptomatic disease states in marine invertebrates; identifies candidate immune and structural biomarkers and microbe–host interaction modules for mechanistic follow-up.

keynotes.title: Multipartite coevolution shapes plant apoplastic immunity against rice blast fungus

keynote.title: Multipartite coevolution shapes plant apoplastic immunity against rice blast fungus
keynote.core_problem: How plant apoplastic immunity evolves in response to fungal effectors, specifically the coevolutionary dynamics between rice and Magnaporthe oryzae involving β-1,3-glucan-binding proteins (GBPs) and thaumatin-like proteins.
contributions: Identifies a multipartite coevolutionary arms race involving rice OsPR5, M. oryzae GBP, fungal thaumatin-binding suppressors, and a rice receptor kinase with a thaumatin-like domain.
Reveals that OsPR5 confers immunity by sequestering fungal GBP from the cell wall.
Demonstrates that fungal secreted thaumatin-binding proteins counteract OsPR5-mediated immunity.
Shows that a rice cell-surface receptor kinase uses a thaumatin-like domain to detect fungal GBP and activate immunity.
Proposes a conserved, thaumatin-based general defense mechanism against fungal pathogens.
methods: Protein-protein interaction assays (e.g., binding studies between OsPR5 and M. oryzae GBP)
Functional genetic analysis (e.g., mutant phenotyping in rice and M. oryzae)
Localization studies (e.g., subcellular localization of GBP and OsPR5 in apoplast)
Evolutionary analysis of thaumatin-like domains across species
experiments_or_evidence: OsPR5 binds M. oryzae GBP and sequesters it from fungal cell wall.
Fungal-secreted thaumatin-binding proteins inhibit OsPR5 function.
Rice receptor kinase with thaumatin-like domain detects GBP and triggers immune activation.
Conservation of thaumatin domain across plant lineages supports generality of mechanism.
assumptions: The apoplastic interaction interface is a primary site for coevolutionary selection.
Thaumatin domain functionality is conserved enough across plants to support a general defense model.

gap_signals.text: Prior work focused on single binary host–pathogen interactions; this study highlights multipartite (≥3-partner), dynamic coevolution in the apoplast.
Previous models did not account for simultaneous immune activation (receptor kinase), effector neutralization (OsPR5), and counter-suppression (fungal thaumatin-binders).

datasets_or_materials: Recombinant OsPR5, GBP, and fungal thaumatin-binding proteins

important_claims.claim: OsPR5 confers immunity by binding and sequestering M. oryzae GBP away from the fungal cell wall.
important_claims.evidence: Direct binding demonstrated; functional consequence shown via loss-of-immunity in OsPR5 mutants and rescue by GBP sequestration assays.

important_claims.claim: Fungal-secreted thaumatin-binding proteins suppress OsPR5-mediated immunity.
important_claims.evidence: Co-expression or exogenous application inhibits OsPR5 function in immunity assays.

important_claims.claim: A rice receptor kinase with a thaumatin-like domain detects fungal GBP to activate immunity.
important_claims.evidence: Genetic and biochemical evidence links receptor kinase activity to GBP recognition and downstream immune signaling.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a template for identifying multipartite coevolution in other plant–pathogen systems; highlights thaumatin-domain proteins as modular, evolvable immune components suitable for engineering broad-spectrum resistance.

This may be caused by genomic complexity and the presence of multiple etiological factors.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: cancer diagnosis and therapy
references: Dipti Bhattacharya et al. (2025) Artificial Intelligence in Cancer Research: Pioneering Biomarker Discovery for Pharmacological Advances doi:10.47583/ijpsrr.2025.v85i5.008

evidence_type_annotations.claim: omics profiling
AI-aided biomarker discovery bridges molecular understanding with clinical application to propel precision pharmacology
Challenges in standardizing data, model interpretation, and ethical implementation; algorithmic bias and restricted diversity in training datasets risk exacerbating healthcare disparities
Full text excerpt:

By merging multi-omics information (genomic, proteomic, transcriptomic) with real-world evidence and clinical records, AI-based algorithms - deep neural networks and ensemble learning models - unveil intricate patterns for anticipating tumor behaviour, drug response, and resistance mechanisms.

For instance, AI has determined biomarkers such as FCN3 and CLEC1B in hepatocellular carcinoma with 93 – 98% diagnostic accuracy, whereas recurrent neural networks (RNNs) have mapped angiogenesis-related therapeutic targets in pan-cancer research.

Significant progress is AI-assisted histopathological image analysis to predict molecular phenotypes and immune checkpoint expression, reducing diagnostic variation by 30 – 40% in validation experiments.

Studies indicate that AI-curated biomarker sets improve detection rates in the early stages by 2% in high-mortality cancers (such as ovarian, pancreatic) and predict responses to immunotherapies with AUC-ROC of up to 0.92.
Algorithmic bias and restricted diversity in training datasets, however, risk exacerbating healthcare disparities, rendering federated learning and sound validation frameworks critical.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: network analysis | UpSet plots | Cox proportional hazards regression
Gender and age-specific characterization of comorbidity and multidrug patterns in HCC provides actionable insights for proactive management and patient-centered care.

To the best of our knowledge, there is no research study investigating age, sex, and CCI stratified network of multimorbidity and multi-drugs exposure, nor exploring the associations between co-comorbidities or co-drugs exposure and follow-up HCC recurrence and mortality risk.

Our gender and age-specific characterization of comorbidity and multidrug patterns in HCC provides clinicians and patients with actionable insights to guide proactive management and patient-centered care, enabling earlier comorbidity detection and prevention strategies.

MethodsThis cohort study comprised 15,998 patients (62.7% male, age at HCC diagnosis: 69.2 [IQR: 58.7-78.7] years old) from the Hong Kong Hospital Authority, enrolled between January 2008 and December 2019 with follow-up until May 2024.
ResultsIn this cohort, 93.0% of patients had at least one pre-existing comorbidity (mean 3.0 per patient), of whom 87.8% (N=13,084) had 1-5 comorbidities.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: liver cancer immunotherapy response
references: Fei-Yu Diao (2021) Single-Cell Landscape of Liver Cancer in Response to Immunotherapy doi:10.4103/apjon.apjon-2165

evidence_type_annotations.claim: single-cell transcriptome analysis

Proposes functional clonality and cell-state heterogeneity—rather than somatic mutation-based clonality—as a more accurate framework for understanding tumor evolution and immunotherapy response in liver cancer.

Existing single-cell analysis techniques cannot interpret tumor clonality and tumor evolution; tumor mutational data from single-cell sequencing yield high false positivity for clonality reconstruction due to prevalence of passenger mutations.
Full text excerpt:
This may be caused by genomic complexity and the presence of multiple etiological factors.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: cancer diagnosis and therapy
references: Dipti Bhattacharya et al. (2025) Artificial Intelligence in Cancer Research: Pioneering Biomarker Discovery for Pharmacological Advances doi:10.47583/ijpsrr.2025.v85i5.008

evidence_type_annotations.claim: omics profiling
AI-aided biomarker discovery bridges molecular understanding with clinical application to propel precision pharmacology
Challenges in standardizing data, model interpretation, and ethical implementation; algorithmic bias and restricted diversity in training datasets risk exacerbating healthcare disparities
Full text excerpt:

By merging multi-omics information (genomic, proteomic, transcriptomic) with real-world evidence and clinical records, AI-based algorithms - deep neural networks and ensemble learning models - unveil intricate patterns for anticipating tumor behaviour, drug response, and resistance mechanisms.

For instance, AI has determined biomarkers such as FCN3 and CLEC1B in hepatocellular carcinoma with 93 – 98% diagnostic accuracy, whereas recurrent neural networks (RNNs) have mapped angiogenesis-related therapeutic targets in pan-cancer research.

Significant progress is AI-assisted histopathological image analysis to predict molecular phenotypes and immune checkpoint expression, reducing diagnostic variation by 30 – 40% in validation experiments.

Studies indicate that AI-curated biomarker sets improve detection rates in the early stages by 2% in high-mortality cancers (such as ovarian, pancreatic) and predict responses to immunotherapies with AUC-ROC of up to 0.92.
Algorithmic bias and restricted diversity in training datasets, however, risk exacerbating healthcare disparities, rendering federated learning and sound validation frameworks critical.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: network analysis | UpSet plots | Cox proportional hazards regression
Gender and age-specific characterization of comorbidity and multidrug patterns in HCC provides actionable insights for proactive management and patient-centered care.

To the best of our knowledge, there is no research study investigating age, sex, and CCI stratified network of multimorbidity and multi-drugs exposure, nor exploring the associations between co-comorbidities or co-drugs exposure and follow-up HCC recurrence and mortality risk.

Our gender and age-specific characterization of comorbidity and multidrug patterns in HCC provides clinicians and patients with actionable insights to guide proactive management and patient-centered care, enabling earlier comorbidity detection and prevention strategies.

MethodsThis cohort study comprised 15,998 patients (62.7% male, age at HCC diagnosis: 69.2 [IQR: 58.7-78.7] years old) from the Hong Kong Hospital Authority, enrolled between January 2008 and December 2019 with follow-up until May 2024.
ResultsIn this cohort, 93.0% of patients had at least one pre-existing comorbidity (mean 3.0 per patient), of whom 87.8% (N=13,084) had 1-5 comorbidities.

Cultural and religious factors were not explored.

Despite marked improvement, persistent geographic and socioeconomic inequities highlight the need for targeted interventions. MethodsWe analyzed data from the Multiple Indicator Cluster Surveys (2012-13 and 2019) and the Bangladesh Demographic and Health Survey (2022), covering 20,770 ever-married women aged 15-49 who gave birth in the preceding two years.
ResultsHome delivery prevalence declined from 68% in 2012-13 to 35% in 2022.
